title: 9 Herbal Creams For Premenstrual Syndrome
intro_desc: Herbal creams can be a natural way to relieve premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms. These creams often contain herbs that have anti-inflammatory and calming properties, which can help to soothe cramps, anxiety, and other symptoms associated with PMS. One example of an herb used in PMS relief creams is Valeriana officinalis, also known as valerian root. This herb is known for its ability to promote relaxation and reduce stress levels, making it easier to fall asleep and manage mood swings.  Lavandula angustifolia, or lavender, is another herb often used in PMS creams. Lavender oil has a calming effect on the body, which can help to reduce anxiety and promote a good night's sleep. Some herbal creams also contain Zingiber officinale, or ginger, which is known for its anti-inflammatory properties. Ginger can help to reduce cramps and ease digestive discomfort, making it a great addition to PMS relief creams.  Using herbal creams for PMS can be beneficial for several reasons. Firstly, they can provide a natural alternative to over-the-counter pain relievers and anti-anxiety medications. Secondly, herbal creams can be applied directly to the skin, allowing the active ingredients to absorb quickly and take effect. This can be especially useful for women who experience cramps and discomfort in the lower abdomen. Additionally, herbal creams can be used in conjunction with other natural remedies, such as heat therapy and relaxation techniques, to create a comprehensive PMS relief plan.  When using herbal creams for PMS, it's essential to choose high-quality products that contain safe and effective ingredients. Look for creams that are free from harsh chemicals and artificial fragrances, and opt for products that have been tested for purity and potency. By incorporating herbal creams into your PMS relief routine, you may find that your symptoms become more manageable, and you're able to enjoy a more comfortable and peaceful premenstrual experience.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis creams contains valerenic acid, isovaleric acid, and valepotriates as its active constituents. These compounds work together to help reduce anxiety and stress, which are common symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). Valerenic acid, in particular, has been shown to interact with the neurotransmitter GABA, promoting relaxation and reducing muscle tension. The valepotriates also have a sedative effect, which can help alleviate insomnia and other sleep disturbances associated with PMS. By reducing anxiety and promoting relaxation, Valeriana officinalis creams can help alleviate the physical and emotional symptoms of PMS.
remedies.image_alt: Valeriana officinalis herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/2 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of shea butter, 2 tablespoons of valerian root oil, and 10 drops of lavender essential oil.
Melt the coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler over low heat. Stir until the beeswax is fully melted.
Add the shea butter to the melted mixture and stir until combined.
Remove the mixture from the heat and stir in the valerian root oil and lavender essential oil.
Pour the mixture into small containers and let it cool and solidify. Store in a cool, dry place and use as needed for premenstrual syndrome relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia creams contains linalool and linalyl acetate, which are the main bioactive constituents. These compounds have a sedative and relaxing effect on the body, helping to reduce anxiety and stress. Lavender's calming properties can also help regulate the body's hormonal balance, which is often disrupted in women experiencing premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The antispasmodic properties of linalool and linalyl acetate can help alleviate menstrual cramps and other physical symptoms associated with PMS. By reducing stress and regulating hormonal balance, Lavandula angustifolia creams may help alleviate some of the symptoms of PMS.
remedies.image_alt: Lavandula angustifolia herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of distilled water, 1/2 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of shea butter, 2 tablespoons of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of dried Lavandula angustifolia flowers.
Combine distilled water and dried Lavandula angustifolia flowers in a saucepan. Heat on low for 10 minutes, then let it cool.
In a separate saucepan, melt coconut oil and shea butter over low heat. Add beeswax and stir until melted.
Strain the Lavandula angustifolia mixture and add it to the melted coconut oil mixture. Stir well.
Pour the mixture into a container and let it cool and solidify. Once solid, use as a cream for premenstrual syndrome relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale creams contains active constituents like gingerol and shogaol, which have anti-inflammatory properties that help alleviate menstrual cramps and discomfort associated with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). These compounds work by blocking the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause pain and inflammation in the uterus. Ginger's antioxidant properties also help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the body, further contributing to its pain-relieving effects. Additionally, ginger has been shown to have a relaxing effect on the muscles, which can help ease tension and cramping. By reducing inflammation, pain, and tension, Zingiber officinale creams can provide relief from the symptoms of PMS.
remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of Zingiber officinale root powder, and 2 tablespoons of shea butter.
Melt coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a microwave-safe bowl in 10-second increments, stirring between each interval, until smooth.
In a separate bowl, mix Zingiber officinale root powder and shea butter until well combined.
Add the powder mixture to the melted coconut oil and beeswax mixture, and stir until smooth.
Pour the mixture into a container, let it cool, and once set, your Zingiber officinale cream is ready for use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Passiflora incarnata creams contains flavonoids, alkaloids, and glycosides as its bioactive constituents. These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS) such as bloating and breast tenderness. The flavonoids in Passiflora incarnata creams have a relaxing effect on the nervous system, reducing anxiety and stress that often accompany PMS. The alkaloids, particularly harmine and harmaline, have a sedative effect, which can help regulate mood swings and improve sleep quality. By reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation, Passiflora incarnata creams may provide relief from PMS symptoms, promoting a sense of calm and well-being.
remedies.image_alt: Passiflora incarnata herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Passiflora incarnata leaves, 1 cup of coconut oil, and 1 tablespoon of beeswax.
Clean and dry the Passiflora incarnata leaves, then chop them into small pieces.
Combine the chopped leaves and coconut oil in a saucepan, and heat it over low heat for 2 hours, stirring occasionally.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter to get the liquid extract. Discard the solids.
Mix the liquid extract with the melted beeswax, then pour the mixture into small containers. Let it cool and harden.

remedies.remedy_desc: Vitex agnus-castus creams contains iridoid glycosides, particularly aucubin and agnuside, which are responsible for its therapeutic effects. These compounds have a regulating effect on the hormonal balance, particularly the luteinizing hormone, which helps to normalize the menstrual cycle. The anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of Vitex agnus-castus also contribute to its beneficial effects on premenstrual syndrome. By reducing inflammation and oxidative stress, the cream can help alleviate symptoms such as breast tenderness, mood swings, and fatigue. Vitex agnus-castus creams also has a sedative effect, which can help regulate sleep patterns and reduce anxiety.
remedies.image_alt: Vitex agnus-castus herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of dried Vitex agnus-castus flowers, and 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil.
Melt the coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Add the dried Vitex agnus-castus flowers to a small muslin bag or a coffee filter and tie it securely.
Steep the herb bag in the melted oil mixture for 2-3 hours. Then, remove the bag and squeeze it gently to release the oil.
Strain the oil mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container. Add the vitamin E oil and mix well. Pour the mixture into small jars and let it cool before use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Avena sativa creams contains a concentrated amount of bioactive constituents, including avenanthramides, ferulic acid, and avenacosides. These compounds have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling and pain associated with premenstrual syndrome. Avenanthramides, in particular, have been shown to have a relaxing effect on the uterus, helping to ease menstrual cramps and discomfort. The ferulic acid in Avena sativa creams also has antioxidant properties, which can help protect the body from oxidative stress and inflammation. By reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation, Avena sativa creams can help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, such as bloating, breast tenderness, and mood swings.
remedies.image_alt: Avena sativa herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Avena sativa (oat straw), 1 cup of carrier oil (coconut or olive oil), and a clean glass jar.
Combine 1/2 cup of dried Avena sativa with 1 cup of carrier oil in a saucepan. Heat on low for 2-3 hours, then let cool.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into the clean glass jar. Discard the solids.
Add 2 tablespoons of beeswax and 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil to the jar. Stir until the beeswax is melted.
Let the mixture cool and solidify. Once set, your Avena sativa cream is ready to use. Store in the fridge for up to 2 weeks.

remedies.remedy_desc: Angelica archangelica creams contains a compound called sesquiterpene, specifically angelan and other sesquiterpenes, which have anti-inflammatory properties. These properties can help reduce swelling and ease cramping associated with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). Angelica archangelica also contains a flavonoid called kaempferol, which has antioxidant properties that can help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the body. Additionally, the creams contain a sesquiterpene called bornyl acetate, which has a sedative effect that can help calm the nervous system and reduce anxiety symptoms often experienced during PMS. The combination of these compounds in Angelica archangelica creams may help alleviate some of the symptoms of PMS.
remedies.image_alt: Angelica archangelica herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Angelica archangelica roots and 2 cups of carrier oil (coconut or olive oil).
Combine the dried roots and carrier oil in a saucepan. Heat the mixture over low heat for 2-3 hours, or until the oil turns greenish.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl. Discard the solids.
Add 1 tablespoon of beeswax and 1 tablespoon of vitamin E oil to the oil mixture. Heat the mixture over low heat, stirring constantly, until the beeswax is fully melted.
Pour the mixture into small containers and let it cool and solidify. Store the creams in a cool, dark place.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis creams contains active constituents such as rosmarinic acid, melissic acid, and linalool, which have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. These properties help to relieve cramps and tension associated with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The rosmarinic acid in Melissa officinalis creams also has antioxidant properties, which can help to reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the body. Additionally, the antispasmodic properties of linalool can help to relax the uterine muscles and reduce menstrual cramps. By reducing inflammation and relaxing the uterine muscles, Melissa officinalis creams can provide relief from PMS symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Melissa officinalis herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of Melissa officinalis flowers, 1/2 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of shea butter, and 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil.
Steep the Melissa officinalis flowers in a cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes, then let it cool. Strain the mixture and set it aside.
Melt the coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of boiling water.
Once the mixture is melted, add the cooled Melissa officinalis infusion, shea butter, and vitamin E oil. Stir until well combined.
Pour the mixture into a container and let it cool and solidify. Once set, your Melissa officinalis cream is ready to use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cimicifuga racemosa creams contains the bioactive constituents cimicifugoside, cimicifugic acid, and actein. These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which can help ease physical symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, such as cramps and bloating. Cimicifugoside and cimicifugic acid have also been shown to have a sedative effect, which can help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation. Actein is a phytoestrogen that can help balance hormone levels, which may help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, such as mood swings and irritability. By addressing these physical and emotional symptoms, Cimicifuga racemosa creams may provide relief for women experiencing premenstrual syndrome.
remedies.image_alt: Cimicifuga racemosa herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of dried Cimicifuga racemosa root, 1/2 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil, and 2 tablespoons of lavender oil.
Grind the dried Cimicifuga racemosa root into a fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle.
Melt the coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Add the ground Cimicifuga racemosa root, vitamin E oil, and lavender oil to the melted coconut oil and beeswax mixture. Stir well.
Remove the mixture from heat and let it cool. Pour the mixture into small tin containers and let it solidify. Once solid, your Cimicifuga racemosa creams are ready to use.

faq_prevent: Drinking herbal tea may help ease premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms. Peppermint and chamomile teas can calm anxiety and soothe stomach issues. Ginger tea can reduce nausea and cramps. These teas have natural chemicals that may help regulate hormones and ease PMS symptoms. Some people find them helpful in managing their symptoms.
faq_safe: Herbal teas can be a helpful way to ease premenstrual syndrome symptoms. However, drinking them every day may not be the best approach. Some teas, like those with high caffeine content, can disrupt sleep and worsen symptoms. Others, like those with estrogenic properties, can cause issues if consumed regularly. Moderation is key.
faq_timeline: Herbal teas can help with premenstrual syndrome, but how long it takes to see results varies. Some people notice a difference within a few days, while others might take a week or two to feel the effects. It's also possible that you might need to try different teas before finding one that works for you.
faq_time: Drinking herbal tea in the evening can help with premenstrual syndrome symptoms. As the body prepares for a good night's sleep, a soothing tea can calm the mind and body. Peppermint, chamomile, or lavender tea can be especially helpful, promoting relaxation and reducing anxiety.
faq_interaction: Herbal teas for premenstrual syndrome can interact with prescription medications. Some herbs, like St. John's Wort, can affect how the body breaks down certain medications, making them stronger or weaker than intended. This can change how well the medications work or increase the risk of side effects. Be cautious when drinking herbal teas with prescription medications.
faq_pregnant: It's best to be cautious when drinking herbal teas while pregnant, especially if you're trying to manage premenstrual symptoms. Some herbal teas can affect the fetus or cause problems during pregnancy. If you're experiencing PMS symptoms, consider speaking with your healthcare provider about safe alternatives.
faq_breastfeeding: Herbal teas for premenstrual syndrome may not be safe for breastfeeding women. Some herbal teas, like those containing sage or peppermint, can affect milk production or pass through to the baby. Others, like those with raspberry leaf, may help with cramps but can also cause stomach issues in some breastfeeding mothers.
title_2: 9 Herbal Creams for Adult Women with Premenstrual Syndrome
